title: Volume word problems
description: Find volume of rectangular prisms to solve word problems.

assessment_items.question_content:
Elaine's jewelry box has a volume of $36$ cubic centimeters.

**Which of the following could be the dimensions of Elaine's jewelry box?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Volume of a rectangular prism can be found by using the formula $\text{Volume}=\text{length}\times \text{width}\times \text{height}$.


Let's plug in the choices and see when we get a volume of $\green{36}$ cubic centimeters.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\pink3\times\pink4\times\pink3=\green{36}$  


[[☃ image 2]]

    
$\gray4\times\gray4\times\gray2=\red{32}$  


[[☃ image 3]]

    
$\blue{12}\times\blue{12}\times\blue{12}=\red{1{,}728}$
The dimensions of Elaine's jewelry box could be the following:

- $3\text{ cm}$ long, $4\text{ cm}$  wide, $3\text{ cm}$  high

choices.content: $3\text{ cm}$ long, $4\text{ cm}$  wide, $3\text{ cm}$  high
$12\text{ cm}$  long, $12\text{ cm}$  wide, $12\text{ cm}$  high
$4\text{ cm}$  long, $4\text{ cm}$  wide, $2\text{ cm}$  high

assessment_items.question_content:
I bought a box from the post office that has a volume of $24$ cubic centimeters.

**Which of the following could be the dimensions of my box?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Volume of a rectangular prism can be found by using the formula $\text{Volume}=\text{length}\times \text{width}\times \text{height}$.


Let's plug in the choices and see when we get a volume of $\green{24}$ cubic centimeters.


[[☃ image 1]]

 
$\pink2\times\pink2\times\pink6=\green{24}$  


[[☃ image 2]]

    
$\gray8\times\gray1\times\gray3=\green{24}$  


[[☃ image 3]]

    
$\blue{10}\times\blue{4}\times\blue{10}=\red{400}$
The dimensions of my box could be the following:

- $2\text{ cm}$ long, $2\text{ cm}$  wide, $6\text{ cm}$  high
- $8\text{ cm}$ long, $1\text{ cm}$  wide, $3\text{ cm}$  high

choices.content: $2\text{ cm}$ long, $2\text{ cm}$  wide, $6\text{ cm}$  high
$8\text{ cm}$ long, $1\text{ cm}$  wide, $3\text{ cm}$  high
$10\text{ cm}$  long, $4\text{ cm}$  wide, $10\text{ cm}$  high

assessment_items.question_content:
Max's pet worm lives in a cardboard box that has a volume of $80$ cubic centimeters.

**Which of the following could be the dimensions of Max's box?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Volume of a rectangular prism can be found by using the formula $\text{Volume}=\text{length}\times \text{width}\times \text{height}$.


Let's plug in the choices and see when we get a volume of $\green{80}$ cubic centimeters.


[[☃ image 1]]

  
$\pink4\times\pink5\times\pink4=\green{80}$  

[[☃ image 2]]

    
$\gray2\times\gray5\times\gray8=\green{80}$  


[[☃ image 3]]

   
$\blue{10}\times\blue{4}\times\blue{2}=\green{80}$
The dimensions of Max's box could be the following:

- $4\text{ cm}$ long, $5\text{ cm}$  wide, $4\text{ cm}$  high
- $2\text{ cm}$ long, $5\text{ cm}$  wide, $8\text{ cm}$  high
- $10\text{ cm}$ long, $4\text{ cm}$  wide, $2\text{ cm}$  high

choices.content: $10\text{ cm}$ long, $4\text{ cm}$  wide, $2\text{ cm}$  high
$4\text{ cm}$  long, $5\text{ cm}$  wide, $4\text{ cm}$  high
$2\text{ cm}$  long, $5\text{ cm}$  wide, $8\text{ cm}$  high

assessment_items.question_content:
A box of granola bars has a volume of $210$ cubic centimeters.

**Which of the following could be the dimensions of the box?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Volume of a rectangular prism can be found by using the formula $\text{Volume}=\text{length}\times \text{width}\times \text{height}$.


Let's plug in the choices and see when we get a volume of $\green{210}$ cubic centimeters.

[[☃ image 1]]

  
$\pink{15}\times\pink2\times\pink7=\green{210}$  

[[☃ image 2]]

   
$\gray7\times\gray3\times\gray{10}=\green{210}$  


[[☃ image 3]]

 
$\blue{14}\times\blue{3}\times\blue{7}=\red{294}$
The dimensions of the box could be the following:

- $15\text{ cm}$ long, $2\text{ cm}$  wide, $7\text{ cm}$  high
- $7\text{ cm}$ long, $3\text{ cm}$  wide, $10\text{ cm}$  high

choices.content: $15\text{ cm}$ long, $2\text{ cm}$  wide, $7\text{ cm}$  high
$7\text{ cm}$  long, $3\text{ cm}$  wide, $10\text{ cm}$  high
$14\text{ cm}$  long, $3\text{ cm}$  wide, $7\text{ cm}$  high

assessment_items.question_content:
A pool is filled with $270$ cubic meters of water.  

The base of the pool is  $15\text{ m}$  long and $9\text{ m}$ wide.  


**What is the height of the water in the pool?** 
 
[[☃ input-number 1]] $\text {m}$
hints: Volume can be found using the formula $\purpleD{\text{Volume}}=\maroonD{\text{length}}\times \blueD{\text{width}}\times \greenD{\text{height}}$.

Let's plug in all the numbers we know:

$\purpleD{270}=\maroonD{15}\times\blueD{9}\times{}\greenD{?}$

$\purpleD{270}=135\times{}\greenD{?}$

What number can we multiply by $135$ to get $\purpleD{270}?$

$\purpleD{270}=135\times\greenD{2}$

The height of the water in the pool is $\greenD{2}$ meters.

assessment_items.question_content:
Layla wants to build a wooden box with a volume of $45$ cubic centimeters.  

She started with a width of $ 3\text{ cm}$  and a height of $ 3\text{ cm}$.  


**How long should Layla make the box?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] $\text {cm}$
hints: Volume can be found using the formula $\purpleD{\text{Volume}}=\maroonD{\text{length}}\times \blueD{\text{width}}\times \greenD{\text{height}}$.

Let's plug in all the numbers we know:

$\purpleD{45}=\greenD{\,?}\times\blueD{3}\times\maroonD{3}$

$\purpleD{45}=\greenD{\,?}\times9$

What number can we multiply by $9$ to get $\purpleD{45}?$

$\purpleD{45}=\greenD5\times9$

Layla should make the box $\greenD5$ centimeters long.

assessment_items.question_content:
I have to build a box for my scouting project with a volume of $36$ cubic centimeters.  

The base of the box is  $ 6\text{ cm}$  long and $ 2\text{ cm}$ wide.  


**How high should I build the box?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] $\text {cm}$
hints: Volume can be found using the formula $\purpleD{\text{Volume}}=\maroonD{\text{length}}\times \blueD{\text{width}}\times \greenD{\text{height}}$.

Let's plug in all the numbers we know:

$\purpleD{36}=\maroonD{6}\times\blueD{2}\times{}\greenD{?}$

$\purpleD{36}=12\times{}\greenD{?}$

What number can we multiply by $12$ to get $\purpleD{36}?$

$\purpleD{36}=12\times\greenD{3}$

The height of the box should be $\green3$ centimeters.

assessment_items.question_content:
Nemo's aquarium is filled with $2{,}400$ cubic centimeters of water.  

The base of the aquarium is  $ 20\text{ cm}$  long and $12\text{ cm}$ wide.  


**What is the height of the water in Nemo's aquarium?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] $\text {cm}$
hints: Volume can be found using the formula $\purpleD{\text{Volume}}=\maroonD{\text{length}}\times \blueD{\text{width}}\times \greenD{\text{height}}$.

Let's plug in all the numbers we know:

$\purple{2{,}400}=\pink{20}\times\blue{12}\times\green{\, ?}$

$\purple{2{,}400}=240\times\green{\, ?}$

What number can we multiply by $240$ to get $\purple{2{,}400}?$

$\purple{2{,}400}=240\times\green{10}$

The height of the water in Nemo's aquarium is $\green{10}$ centimeters.

assessment_items.question_content:
The sea turtle habitat at the zoo is made by connecting two large aquariums. 

The first aquarium is $6\text { m}$  long, $4\text { m}$ wide, and $2\text { m}$ high. 

The second aquarium is $8\text { m}$ long, $9\text { m}$ wide, and $3\text { m}$ high.


**How many cubic meters of space do the sea turtles have in their habitat?**    

[[☃ input-number 1]] cubic meters
hints:
Since the sea turtles can go in both aquariums, we need to add the volumes of the two aquariums.

$\blueD{48}+\maroonD{216}=\greenD{264}$
The sea turtles have $\greenD{264}$ cubic meters of space in their habitat.

assessment_items.question_content:
I made a fort by connecting two boxes. 

The first box is $5$ meters long, $9$ meters wide, and $9$ meters high. 

The second box is $3$ meters long, $8$ meters wide, and $2$ meters high.


**How many cubic meters of space does my fort have?**    

[[☃ input-number 1]] cubic meters
hints:
We need to add the volumes of the two boxes.

$\blueD{405}+\maroonD{48}=\greenD{453}$
My fort has $\greenD{453}$ cubic meters of space.

assessment_items.question_content:
A rectangular-prism-shaped toy chest is $2 \text { m}$ by $1 \text { m}$ by $1 \text { m}$.  

A shipping crate is packed with $18$ of these toy chests. There is no extra space in the crate. 

**What is the volume of the crate?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] cubic meters
hints:
First, let's find the volume of one toy chest.

$\begin{align}
\text{Volume}&=\text{length}\times \text{width}\times \text{height}\\\\
&=2\times1\times1\\\\
&= \blueD{2} \text{ cubic meters}\\\\
\end{align}$

There are $\maroonD{18}$ toy chests in the crate. 

To find the volume of the shipping crate, we multiply the volume of one toy chest $(\blueD{2}$ cubic meters$)$ by the number of toy chests $(\maroonD{18})$.

$\blueD{2}\times\maroonD{18}=\greenD{36}$
The volume of the shipping crate is $\greenD{36}$ cubic meters.

assessment_items.question_content:
Erin built a wooden box to hold hay on her farm.  The box is $3 \text { m}$ long,  $1 \text { m}$ wide, and $1 \text { m}$ high.  

Hay costs $\$14$ per cubic meter.  

**How much will it cost to completely fill the box with hay?**  

$\$$[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
First, let's find the volume of the box.

$V=\text{length}\times \text{width}\times \text{height}$
$V=3\times1\times1$  
$V= \blueD{3} \text{ cubic meters}$

It costs $\maroonD{\$14}$ for each cubic meter of hay.  

So, we need to multiply $\pink{\$14}$ by the number of cubic meters $(\blueD{3})$ in the box.

$\blueD{3} \times \maroonD{\$14} = \greenD{\$42}$
It will cost $\greenD{\$42}$ to completely fill the box with hay.

assessment_items.question_content:
My mom built a new sandbox that is $3 \text { m}$ long,  $2 \text { m}$ wide, and $1 \text { m}$ high for my sister and me.  

Sand for the sandbox costs $\$4.50$ per cubic meter.  

**How much will it cost to completely fill the sandbox with sand?**  

$\$$[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
First, let's find the volume of the sandbox.

$V=\text{length}\times \text{width}\times \text{height}$ 
$V=3\times2\times1$  
$V= \blueD{6} \text{ cubic meters}$

It costs $\maroonD{\$4.50}$ for each cubic meter of sand.  So, we need to multiply $\maroonD{\$4.50}$ by the number of cubic meters $(\blueD{6})$ in the sandbox.

$\blueD{6} \times \maroonD{\$4.50} = \greenD{\$27}$
It will cost $\greenD{\$27}$ to completely fill the sandbox with sand.
